Auria Update

Wenqing Chen
03.12.2024

See the cores that we are interested in.

Check the proportion of patients in each RPD to confirm whether there is a sampling effect.

Try to use all markers to run UTAG, and to compare the differences.

Compare the differences between results with all cells and only cancer cells.

Last meeting

I used the dataset with markers that passed QC to run UTAG, but I find the results have big batch effect, I think it come from slides. And next I used a lot of methods to try to solve this problem, however, it did not work :(

Problems — Batch effect

Data with less markers

Results of UTAG

After running UTAG, it got 183 clusters
Combine 183 UTAG clusters to 14 RPDs based on the marker expression
Column name is RPDs

Problems — Batch effect

Image_ID: INF2

Figure2: Spatial distribution within RPDs

Figure1: a lot of cores in INF2 are clustered to UTAG_cluster_0
Figure2: after combing UTAG clusters to RPDs, almost all cores are annotated to RPD10

UTAG with 30 microns and 0.2 resolution

Results from Scimap

Image_ID: INF2

Spatial count with 45px radius (corresponds to 30 microns)

Figure3: Spatial distribution within RCNs

Figure4: Celltpes distribution within RCNs

Problems — Batch effect

Core_ID: Core_79_INF2

UTAG clusters

Scimap RCNs

Figure5: Tissue microanatomical domains

From figure 5, we can find UTAG did not distinguish core79 very well, but scimap gave very good results.

Problems — Batch effect

Image_ID: 2B

Figure7: Spatial distribution within RPDs

Figure6: Spatial distribution within UTAG clusters

In slide 2B, it also shows the same situation :(

UTAG with 30 microns and 0.2 resolution

Problems — Batch effect

Core_ID: Core_15_2B

UTAG clusters

Scimap RCNs

Figure8: Tissue microanatomical domains

From the UTAG clusters and RPDs distribution, I think the problem is the big differences between slides.

Try to find the solutions

Solution 1 & 2:
Pre-proccess before analysis

1. pyComBat batch correction to phase out sample-specific biases. (https://github.com/epigenelabs/pyComBat)

Solution 3:
Run UTAG per slide and merge together

2. z-score normalization truncated at 3 standard deviations for all data at the same time

2. z-score normalization truncated at 3 standard deviations for all data one by one based on imageID

OR

1. Run the UTAG with 30 microns and 0.2 resolution
2. UTAG clusters with images. e.g. INF2_0, 2B_0

3. Based on the marker expression within UTAG clusters, hope these clusters can mixed well

[ Marker expression data: gated and rescaled ]

Solution 1 & 2

Figure9: PCA figure before pyComBat

pyComBat was used at first to remove sample-specific biases

Figure10: PCA figure after pyComBat

Solution 1

Choose 3 slides to do small test (INF2, 2B, 19_02_02A)

I think it still has big batch effect from different slides.

Figure11: PCA figure after pyComBat for 3 slides

Figure12: slide proportion within UTAG clusters

Solution 2

Choose 3 slides to do small test (INF2, 2B, 19_02_02A)

Same as solution 1 result, the slide can not mix well within each UTAG clusters.

Solution 3

Core_79_INF2

Some UTAG results for INF2 and 2B slides
(Run UTAG one by one)

Core_15_2B

The core_79_INF2 has a good clustering result, but core_15_2B still has no good distinction

Solution 3

Here I want to get a hierarchical tree for these 2 single UTAG Results (slide INF2 and 2B), but it divided into 2 parts, the left part from INF2, and the right part from 2B… :(

Scimap (spatial_lag and spatial_count)

Next, I tried spatial_lag (without annotation) and spatial_count (with annotation) form Scimap.

[ Marker expression data: gated and rescaled ]

Spatial_lag: based on marker expression

Scimap (spatial_lag and spatial_count)

Next, I tried spatial_lag (without annotation) and spatial_count (with annotation) form Scimap.

[ Marker expression data: gated and rescaled ]

Spatial_count: based on celltype annotation

The samples are evenly distributed in each cluster

Data from the UTAG paper

Lung CyCIF dataset

A dataset of 19 CyCIF ROI images (3 slides) with 26 markers from three lung cancer patients.
After running UTAG, it got 21 clusters.
In this paper, it only has one figure for the CyCIF dataset, and only divided into Tumor, Stroma and Low expression.

Data from the UTAG paper

Breast Cancer dataset

In this paper, it doesn’t contain any information about breast cancer, but in its GitHub, we can find one code to do some analysis about breast cancer.
Original raw data available from The Single-Cell Pathology Landscape of Breast Cancer by Jackson et Al. (Nature, 2019)

IMC data with 376 slides (I got this information from the UTAG results file)
Here I only choose the first 5 slide to see the slide proportion within UTAG clusters

Conclusion

For the UTAG method, it can get good results if we run it per slide. However, if we run all data at the same time, it will show big batch effect (from slides). We can see the same situation from Spatial_lag (Scimap). So I think maybe this is the problem for all methods of spatial analysis using marker expression data.

I tried different ways to normalize the expression data, but it did not work.

I checked with Inga-Maria, she used spatial_count with a 45px radius that corresponds to 30 microns.

Next ?
Use the raw data (it means the data without gated and rescaled), followed by log_transformation —> z-score normalization truncated at positive and negative 3 standard deviations —> Combat batch correction —> z-score again [From the paper]
Use the raw data, followed by log2-transformed protein marker expression data —> scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score) —> cells with marker and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded (based on whole TMA dataset ~900 cores) [From my colleague]
Because we can get good UTAG results when we focus on one image, maybe we can get the Scimap results at first, and then to select the interested cores to do UTAG analysis ? (a little strange, I'm not sure if this would be a valid analysis, or if this would be an unnecessary step)
Back to Scimap (spatial count) analysis.
